items.q: What is the significance of Duchamp's 'Fountain'?
items.correct: It challenged the definition of art - the artist's choice, not their skill, becomes the artwork.
items.random_wrong: It is a study of plumbing.
items.why: Students miss the conceptual point. Duchamp didn't make the urinal - he selected and presented it. The radical claim is that ART IS DESIGNATION, opening the door to all conceptual art.

items.q: What is the central innovation of Cubism?
items.correct: Showing an object from multiple viewpoints simultaneously on a flat surface.
items.why: Students take the name literally. Cubism is named for facet-like fragmentation, but it isn't about cubes - it's about breaking the single Renaissance viewpoint into many at once.

items.q: What attitude does Pop Art take toward consumer culture?
items.correct: Ambiguous - it both celebrates and critiques mass media imagery.
items.why: Students read Pop Art as either pure celebration or pure protest. Warhol's deadpan repetition of Marilyn or soup cans is deliberately AMBIGUOUS, leaving viewers to decide if it's homage or critique.

items.q: Which photographer coined the phrase 'decisive moment'?
items.why: It's Henri Cartier-Bresson. “Robert Capa” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which setting most directly controls depth of field?
items.why: It's Aperture. “ISO” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Approximately what f-stop is considered a wide aperture providing shallow depth of field?
items.why: Exact answer: 2 f-stop. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
